solutions.solution: Inference: I. Whenever the taxes are raised, the collections improve. II. The collections will not improve if the taxes are not raised. Explanation: The first statement says 'only if' implying that the conditions are that the tax is raised is 'necessary' but the use of the word 'may' shows that it is not 'sufficient' for the collections to improve. In other words, the sentence implies that collections are not going to improve without tax rise, while if taxes were hidden raised, it may or may not improve. Consequent to this, the evaluations of the three inferences are: I. data inadequate II. definitely false III. definitely true

Contextual key words In the preceding page, we have studied some standard key words and their use in context. Their meaning and importance are fixed in nature. The contextual words are normal to these. Sometimes, as a general, common words play the role of key words because of the context in which they are used. And consequently, they give an inference a definite degree of truth (or falsity). Let us see some examples to understand these. Ex 27: Passage: Refer to sample passage 3. Inference: Privatisation has been practiced in the USA since long. Explanation: The passage says that privatisation has been sweeping both the developed and the developing countries for more than a decade. Now, 'developed' and 'more than a decade' are key words here because the context in which they have been used will prove useful in evaluating the given inference. We know that 'USA is a developed nation and we may further assume that a period of more than a decade may be treated as 'since long'. Hence, the given inference is probably true. It is not definitely true because concrete evidence is not available.

Ex 28: Passage: Refer to sample passage 7. Inference: There will not be a single accident on roads if they are satisfactorily illuminated. Explanation: The passage talks of accidents and holds the poor state of street-lighting responsible for this state. Naturally, then, we would expect that an inference will be true if it says that proper illumination will reduce the number of accidents. But the inference given above makes an overstatement. It says 'not a single accident will occur. This is very unlikely. If lights were the only solution, then not a single road accident would occur during day time. The inference is probably false.

How to avoid confusion while deriving inferences We have seen that a given inference can, sometimes, be very easily evaluated because it is almost explicitly stated in the passage. At other times, we may have to apply a little logic while evaluating a given inference because inference may be only indirectly related to the passage. While doing this, we need to pay some special attention to some key words because they may provide us key to the answer. But we are likely to get confused sometimes, especially if the questions are very tough. Actually stated there are four main possibilities of confusion. We are giving some tips so that you overcome the confusion easily. 1. Definitely True or Probably True? This confusion may arise if the given inference is not directly stated in the passage and yet appears 'almost' definitely true to you. But since it is not directly or explicitly stated you hesitate and think that even 'probably true' could be correct. To avoid this confusion recheck your reasoning. If the inference is not mentioned directly in the passage, then you must have assumed something.
